serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Jhalfrezi is approximately 200 g (about 1 cup), as commonly plated in Singaporean Indian restaurants alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Jhalfrezi — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Jhalfrezi (cr_id G00010680). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton cooked composite (NDB 17174) as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Vegetable Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-GI Protein-Dominant Composite Dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Jhalfrezi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant meat-and-vegetable composite dishes with tomato-based sauce. Carbohydrate content is low (7.44 g/100g), predominantly from tomatoes, onions, and bell peppers, which are low-GI vegetables. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 5.2 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 934 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles, papadums, or salted chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 7.44 g/100g and sugar 4.69 g/100g are well below diabetes thresholds; high protein (14.05 g/100g) supports satiety and moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Jhalfrezi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the low carbohydrate load and high protein content support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~4.8 g/100g and cholesterol 85 mg/100g; sodium 467 mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Jhalfrezi to small portions (100–150 g), choose lean mutton cuts, request reduced oil and salt in preparation, and avoid frequent consumption given the combined saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; nightshade vegetables (tomato, bell pepper, green chilli) may trigger individual flares; sodium 467 mg/100g exceeds RA-safe thresholds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Jhalfrezi due to the pro-inflammatory omega-6 content of red meat and the presence of nightshade vegetables; substituting mutton with chicken or fish and requesting reduced salt are recommended if the dish is consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Jhalfrezi is a popular Indian dish consisting of tender mutton pieces cooked in a semi-dry, tomato-based gravy with onions, bell peppers, green chillies, ginger, and garlic. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Singaporean Indian restaurants as a main course alongside rice or flatbreads such as roti prata or naan. The cooking method involves sautéing the spiced mutton with aromatics and vegetables over high heat, producing a dish with a bold, spicy, and slightly tangy flavour profile and a firm, meaty texture. Nutritionally, Mutton Jhalfrezi provides 192 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 14.05 g, moderate fat of 11.58 g, and low carbohydrate of 7.44 g, making it a protein-dense, low-carbohydrate dish. The sodium content is elevated at 467 mg/100g, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and sugar content and high protein, but should be paired with low-GI accompaniments. The presence of red meat (mutton) and nightshade vegetables makes it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with certified halal mutton.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mutton Jhalfrezi?
Is Mutton Jhalfrezi suitable for diabetics?
Is Mutton Jhalfrezi high in sodium?
Can I eat Mutton Jhalfrezi if I have high blood pressure?
What vegetables are in Mutton Jhalfrezi?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010680, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked mutton/lamb composite as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Mutton Jhalfrezi preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked lamb/mutton composite (NDB 17174) and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Jhalfrezi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Chinese local name (羊肉炒辣椒) is a descriptive translation meaning 'stir-fried lamb/mutton with chilli'; no standardised Chinese name for Jhalfrezi is established in common usage. Halal status assumes mutton sourced from a certified halal supplier.
